exercises.title: The three NSA branches
exercises.prompt: DeepSeek's Native Sparse Attention (NSA) has three parallel attention branches combined by a learned gate. Name each branch, what it captures, and why three branches together beat any single sparse pattern.
exercises.hint: Compressed/coarse tokens (global gist), selected fine-grained tokens (the important ones, full detail), sliding window (local context). A learned gate weights them per query. One pattern alone misses something.
exercises.solution:
**The three branches:**
1. **Compressed (coarse-grained) attention:** the long history is **compressed into a smaller number of coarse 'block' tokens** (each summarizing a chunk of the sequence). This branch gives the query a **cheap global overview** of the entire context — the gist of far-away history without attending to every token.
2. **Selected (fine-grained) attention:** the model **selectively retains the most important blocks/tokens** and attends to them at **full detail**. This recovers the precise information that compression would blur — the 'needle' tokens that actually matter for this query.
3. **Sliding-window (local) attention:** attends to a **fixed window of recent neighbors** at full resolution, capturing **local context** (which is usually the most relevant for the next token).

A **learned gate** combines the three branch outputs **per query** (and per head), letting the model decide how much to weight global gist vs selected detail vs local context for each token.

**Why three beat one:** each single sparse pattern has a blind spot. **Sliding-window alone** loses true long-range dependencies (it only sees nearby tokens). **Compression alone** captures global structure but **blurs the fine detail** needed for exact retrieval. **Selection alone** needs *something* to decide what's important and can miss local/global context. By running **all three in parallel and gating them**, NSA simultaneously covers **local (window) + global-coarse (compressed) + global-fine (selected)** — so it doesn't have to choose between cheap-but-blurry and precise-but-expensive. It approximates full attention's coverage while attending to far fewer tokens, which is why it matches or beats full-attention *quality* at long context while being much cheaper.

exercises.title: Why 'natively trainable' matters
exercises.prompt: Prior sparse-attention methods were mostly inference-only (bolted on after training). Explain why NSA being 'natively trainable' (end-to-end differentiable, used during pre-training) is the key advance, and what goes wrong when you sparsify only at inference. Compute NSA's compute savings vs full attention at 64k context.
exercises.hint: Inference-only sparsity creates a train/inference mismatch: the model trained on full attention is forced into a sparse pattern it never learned → quality drops. NSA trains with the sparsity (differentiable selection/gate) so the model learns to use it. Savings: full = O(N²); NSA ≈ O(N·(compressed + selected·block + window)).
exercises.solution:
**Why native trainability is the key advance:** if you **train with full attention** and then **sparsify only at inference** (the prior approach), you create a **train/inference distribution mismatch** — the model learned to rely on attending to *everything*, and you suddenly force it through a sparse pattern it **never saw during training**. The result is **quality degradation**: the model's learned attention behavior assumed access to tokens the sparse pattern now drops, so retrieval and reasoning suffer. You're approximating a computation the model wasn't trained for.
**NSA trains *with* the sparsity:** the three-branch structure — including the **block selection** and the **gating** — is **end-to-end differentiable**, so it's used **during pre-training**. The model **learns to use the sparse structure**: it learns *which* blocks to compress, *which* to select, and *how* to gate them, so its weights are adapted to the sparse pattern from the start. No mismatch — the sparse attention *is* the attention the model was trained on. This is why NSA **matches or beats full-attention quality** where bolt-on sparse methods lost quality. (Making the discrete 'select top-k blocks' step differentiable/trainable is the technical crux — NSA designs the selection so gradients flow.)

**Compute savings at 64k context:** full attention is **O(N²)** — every query attends to all N=64k keys. NSA's cost per query is roughly the **sum of its three branches**, each far smaller than N:
- **compressed:** `N / block_size` coarse tokens (e.g. 64k/64 ≈ 1k),
- **selected:** `top_k` blocks × `block_size` tokens (e.g. 16 blocks × 64 ≈ 1k),
- **window:** `W` local tokens (e.g. 512).
```python
def attn_cost_full(N): return N*N
def attn_cost_nsa(N, block=64, topk=16, window=512):
    compressed = N // block
    selected   = topk * block
    return N * (compressed + selected + window)   # per-query work × N queries
N = 64_000
print(attn_cost_full(N), attn_cost_nsa(N))   # ~4.1e9  vs  ~1.6e8
```
So each query attends to ~**(1k + 1k + 512) ≈ 2.5k** tokens instead of **64k** — roughly a **25× reduction** in attention work at 64k context, and the gap **grows with N** (full is quadratic, NSA is ~linear in N for fixed branch sizes). Combined with **hardware-aligned, kernel-friendly** block structure, this is why NSA **runs faster than FlashAttention on 64k decodes** while keeping quality — the rare sparse method that wins on *both* speed and quality because it's trained natively (ACL 2025 best paper).

exercises.title: NSA in the long-context efficiency landscape
exercises.prompt: At 64k tokens attention eats 70-80% of decode latency, so every lab has a long-context plan. Compare NSA to the alternatives (FlashAttention, sliding-window/interleaved, MLA/GQA KV compression, linear/SSM attention, differential attention) on what axis each attacks (compute vs memory vs quality), why hardware-alignment matters, and how these techniques compose in a 2026 frontier model.
exercises.hint: FlashAttention: memory (still O(N²) compute). Sliding/sparse: compute. MLA/GQA: KV cache memory. SSM (Jamba): linear-time + constant memory but quality gap. Differential: quality/noise. NSA: compute + quality + hardware-aligned + trainable. They compose along different axes.
exercises.solution:
**The problem:** at 64k context, **attention dominates decode latency (70-80%)** because it's O(N²) compute *and* the KV cache it reads is huge. Different techniques attack **different axes**:

| Technique | Axis attacked | What it does | Cost / limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FlashAttention** | **activation memory** (and kernel speed) | tiles + online softmax so the N×N matrix never hits HBM → O(N) memory, exact | **still O(N²) compute** — doesn't reduce work, just memory traffic |
| **Sliding-window / interleaved** (Gemma, Mistral) | **compute** | attend to a local window (+occasional global layers) → O(N·W) | pure-local loses direct long-range; needs global layers |
| **MLA / GQA** | **KV-cache memory/bandwidth** | fewer/compressed KV heads → smaller cache | doesn't reduce attention compute per token |
| **Linear attention / SSM** (Mamba, Jamba) | **compute + memory** | recurrence → linear time, constant memory | historically a **quality gap** vs full attention |
| **Differential attention** (Lesson 16) | **quality (noise floor)** | difference of softmaxes cancels noise → better long-context retrieval | doesn't reduce token count/cost |
| **NSA** | **compute + quality**, hardware-aligned, **trainable** | 3 branches (compressed/selected/window) + gate → ~linear compute, matches full quality | needs native training; selection complexity |

**What makes NSA distinctive:** it's one of the few that wins on **compute *and* quality *and* speed** simultaneously — because it's **trained natively** (no train/inference mismatch, Exercise 2) and **hardware-aligned**. 

**Why hardware-alignment matters:** a sparse pattern that's mathematically efficient but **irregular** (scattered, per-token-varying memory accesses) maps **badly to GPUs**, which want **coalesced, block-structured, dense-matmul-friendly** memory access. Many theoretically-cheaper sparse methods are **slower in practice** than dense FlashAttention because they thrash memory and underuse tensor cores. NSA deliberately uses **block-structured** compression/selection so its sparse ops compile to **efficient, kernel-friendly** GPU operations — which is why it can actually **beat FlashAttention wall-clock** at 64k, not just on paper. 'Theoretical FLOPs saved' is worthless if the kernel can't realize it; **hardware-alignment is what converts sparsity into real speedup.**

**How they compose in a 2026 frontier model (different axes → stackable):** these aren't competitors so much as **complementary layers of the stack**:
- **NSA (or sliding/sparse)** cuts **attention compute** (which tokens you attend to),
- implemented **on a FlashAttention-style kernel** for **memory efficiency**,
- with **MLA/GQA + KV quantization** shrinking the **KV cache** (memory/bandwidth),
- optionally **differential attention** improving **quality/noise** within the attention,
- and at the macro level, **hybrid SSM-Transformer** designs (Jamba, Lesson 21) replace *most* layers with **linear-time SSM** and keep a few attention layers for quality.
A real long-context model might run: **mostly NSA/sparse attention layers (compute) with MLA KV compression (memory) on Flash kernels, a few full/global layers (long-range quality), and SSM layers (linear-time) interleaved** — each technique handling the axis it's best at. The unifying 2026 lesson: **long-context efficiency is a multi-axis problem (compute, KV memory, bandwidth, quality, hardware-utilization), and the winning systems combine specialized, hardware-aligned, *natively-trained* techniques rather than seeking one silver bullet.** NSA is the standout because it's hardware-aligned and trainable, attacking compute and quality at once.
